cards.front: Why is it recommended to use a global Mersenne Twister engine instead of local static ones?
cards.back: A global engine avoids the static initialization order problem, ensures consistent seeding, and can be shared across the entire program.

cards.concept: Creating a Random namespace for random utilities
cards.explanation:
Best practice is to create a Random namespace containing:
  • A global mt19937 engine (seeded with random_device)
  • Helper functions for common random operations
  • Put in a header file (e.g., Random.h)
  • This centralizes random number generation
  • Avoids duplicate engines and inconsistent seeding
examples:
namespace Random
{
    inline std::mt19937 mt{std::random_device{}()};
    
    int get(int min, int max) { ... }
}

cards.text: To define a global random engine in a header without ODR violations, use the {{c1::inline}} keyword.

cards.question: How do you declare a global mt19937 engine in a header file?
cards.explanation: The inline keyword allows the definition in the header without violating the One Definition Rule when included in multiple files.

cards.front: What is the advantage of creating helper functions like Random::get(min, max)?
cards.back: It simplifies random number generation - users don't need to understand distributions, just call a simple function with the desired range.

cards.text:
A helper function for random integers in a range:
int get(int min, int max)
{
    {{c1::std::uniform_int_distribution die{min, max};}}
    {{c2::return die(mt);}}
}

cards.front: How would you call a random number function from a Random namespace?
cards.back: Random::get(1, 6) - where get is the helper function that returns a random number in the specified range.

cards.concept: Benefits of a centralized Random namespace
cards.explanation:
Centralizing random utilities in a namespace provides:
  • Single point of seeding (one engine for entire program)
  • Consistent random number quality
  • Easy to use helper functions
  • No duplicate engines or initialization
  • Can add more random utilities in one place
  • Better than scattered static local engines
examples:
// Easy to use
int roll = Random::get(1, 6);
int percent = Random::get(0, 100);

cards.front: Why should the Random namespace and engine be in a header file?
cards.back: So it can be included in any source file that needs random numbers, making it available throughout the program.

cards.front: How can you make random number generation testable/reproducible?
cards.back: Add a seed() function to the Random namespace that allows setting a specific seed for the mt19937 engine, useful for debugging and testing.
